claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to understand a bit about your situation.

**First, let me ask about the child you're claiming for:**

1. **What is your child's name?** (This helps me keep track if you have multiple children)
2. **How old is your child?** This determines which eligibility path we follow:
   - Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
   - Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
   - Children aged 20 or over typically don't qualify

Please tell me about each child you'd like to claim Child Benefit for, starting with their name and age.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, thanks for helping me with this! 

I'm looking to claim for my son Alex. He's 16 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thanks for that information! So you're looking to claim for your son **Alex**, who is **16 years old** and lives at home with you.

Since Alex is 16, he falls into the 16-19 age category, which means his eligibility depends on whether he's in approved education or training.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to understand Alex's current education or training situation. For 16-19 year olds, there are different scenarios:

**Is Alex currently:**

1. **In approved education or training?** (e.g., at school, college, sixth form, or on an approved training course)

2. **Recently left education or training?** (within the last few months - there may be an extension period where you can still claim)

3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave

Which of these applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh yes, Alex is still in education. He's studying for his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, which is approved education. Let me check the education level requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! A-levels are **approved (non-advanced) education**, which means they qualify for Child Benefit purposes. 

Now I need to check a few more details about Alex's education and work situation:

1. **Is Alex's A-level course full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study - most sixth form A-level courses are full-time)

2. **Is this course funded by an employer**, or does Alex have an agreement with an employer for a future job as part of this course? (Like an apprenticeship arrangement)

3. **Does Alex work?** If yes, is it full-time work (16+ hours per week)?

4. **Is Alex married or in a civil partnership?** (This only matters if the answer is yes, as married 16-19 year olds must be financially dependent on you to qualify)

Please let me know about these points!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, it's a full-time course at the college - he's there most days doing his studies.

No, it's not funded by an employer or anything like that - it's just regular sixth form.

He doesn't work at all, actually. He's focused on his studies.

And no, he's not married or in a civil partnership - he's just 16!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! That all checks out beautifully:

- ✓ Full-time A-level course (approved education)
- ✓ Not employer-funded
- ✓ Not working
- ✓ Not married

Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Since Alex doesn't work at all, he's definitely working less than 24 hours per week, so that's fine!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question: **Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?** 

Specifically, benefits like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Income Support

Or is he NOT receiving any of these types of benefits?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not receiving any benefits in his own name. He's just a student living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex isn't receiving any benefits in his own right.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Just to confirm - **is Alex doing an apprenticeship?**

You mentioned he's doing A-levels at sixth form, so I assume he's not in an apprenticeship, but I just need to confirm this as apprenticeships have different rules depending on the region (England, Scotland, or Wales).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not doing an apprenticeship. He's just doing regular A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is not in an apprenticeship - he's doing regular A-levels. Let me move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check **your residency status** (as the claimant). 

**Do you normally live in the UK?** 

If you do, that's straightforward and we can move on. 

If not, there are some special circumstances that might apply (like having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or being a Crown servant abroad), but most people claiming Child Benefit simply live in the UK normally.

Which applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK. I live here with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You normally live in the UK with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to confirm **responsibility for Alex**. You mentioned Alex lives at home with you, which means you meet the responsibility requirement.

Just to confirm: **Do you live with Alex?** (which you've already indicated you do)

This is one of the main ways to show responsibility - either living with the child OR contributing to their upkeep financially.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You live with Alex, so you meet the responsibility requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now an important question: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. If someone else is claiming, you would need to agree with them to transfer the claim to you.

Is anyone else claiming for Alex, or would you be the only claimant?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. I would be the only claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! You're the only person claiming for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Alex's living situation. You mentioned he lives at home with you, but I need to confirm there are no special circumstances:

**Is Alex:**
- In hospital (currently staying in hospital for medical treatment)?
- In care (placed in local authority care)?
- Fostered?
- Or just living normally at home with you?

Which applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's just living normally at home with me. None of those special circumstances apply.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is living normally at home with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Just one final check: **Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs?**

For example, are they contributing to his housing or upkeep? 

Or is the council NOT paying anything - you're supporting Alex yourself?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything. I'm supporting Alex myself - he just lives at home with me and I pay for everything.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! The council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - you're supporting him yourself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit! 

Based on all the information you've provided, **you meet all the eligibility requirements** for Child Benefit for your son Alex.

### Why You're Eligible:

✓ **Alex qualifies as a child** - He's 16 years old and in approved full-time education (A-levels)  
✓ **Approved education** - A-levels at sixth form are non-advanced, approved qualifications  
✓ **Not working full-time** - Alex isn't working at all  
✓ **No disqualifying benefits** - Alex isn't receiving benefits in his own right  
✓ **Not in an apprenticeship** - He's doing regular A-levels  
✓ **You meet residency requirements** - You normally live in the UK  
✓ **You're responsible for Alex** - He lives at home with you  
✓ **No competing claims** - No one else is claiming for Alex  
✓ **No council support** - The council isn't paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of it back through tax
5. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important Note About High Earners:
If you or your partner earns between £60,000-£80,000, you'll face a tax charge. If either of you earns over £80,000, the charge equals the full Child Benefit amount. You can still claim but opt not to receive payments if you prefer.

You can apply here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 16 years old, within the 16-19 age bracket that can qualify; (2) He is in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college with 12+ hours/week supervised study); (3) A-levels are non-advanced, approved qualifications; (4) The course is not employer-funded; (5) He is not working (therefore working less than 24 hours/week); (6) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (7) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (8) The claimant normally lives in the UK; (9) The claimant lives with Alex, demonstrating responsibility; (10) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (11) Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (12) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their 16-year-old son Alex, who is in approved full-time A-level education and living at home with the claimant who supports him.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 16 years old, within the 16-19 age bracket that can qualify; (2) He is in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college with 12+ hours/week supervised study); (3) A-levels are non-advanced, approved qualifications; (4) The course is not employer-funded; (5) He is not working (therefore working less than 24 hours/week); (6) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (7) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (8) The claimant normally lives in the UK; (9) The claimant lives with Alex, demonstrating responsibility; (10) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (11) Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (12) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their 16-year-old son Alex, who is in approved full-time A-level education and living at home with the claimant who supports him.
